coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots: Recent developments in its fluorescent sensor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Munusamy, Sathishkumar
Mandlimath, Triveni Rajashekhar
Swetha, Puchakayala
Al-Sehemi, Abdullah G.
Pannipara, Mehboobali
Koppala, Sivasankar
Shanmugam, Paramasivam
Boonyuen, Supakorn
Pothu, Ramyakrishna
Boddula, Rajender
coredata.dc:description: Doped carbon dots have attracted great attention from researchers across disciplines because of their unique characteristics, such as their low toxicity, physiochemical stability, photostability, and outstanding biocompatibility. Nitrogen is one of the most commonly used elements for doping because of its sizeable atomic radius, strong electronegativity, abundance, and availability of electrons. This distinguishes them from other atoms and allows them to perform distinctive roles in various applications. Here, we have reviewed the most current breakthroughs in nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) for fluorescent sensor applications in the last five years. The first section of the article addresses several synthetic and sustainable ways of making N-CDs. Next, we briefly reviewed the fluorescent features of N-CDs and their sensing mechanism. Furthermore, we have thoroughly reviewed their fluorescent sensor applications as sensors for cations, anions, small molecules, enzymes, antibiotics, pathogens, explosives, and pesticides. Finally, we have discussed the N-CDs' potential future as primary research and how that may be used. We hope that this study will contribute to a better understanding of the principles of N-CDs and the sensory applications that they can serve.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doped carbon dots have attracted great attention from researchers across disciplines because of their unique characteristics, such as their low toxicity, physiochemical stability, photostability, and outstanding biocompatibility. Nitrogen is one of the most commonly used elements for doping because of its sizeable atomic radius, strong electronegativity, abundance, and availability of electrons. This distinguishes them from other atoms and allows them to perform distinctive roles in various applications. Here, we have reviewed the most current breakthroughs in nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) for fluorescent sensor applications in the last five years. The first section of the article addresses several synthetic and sustainable ways of making N-CDs. Next, we briefly reviewed the fluorescent features of N-CDs and their sensing mechanism. Furthermore, we have thoroughly reviewed their fluorescent sensor applications as sensors for cations, anions, small molecules, enzymes, antibiotics, pathogens, explosives, and pesticides. Finally, we have discussed the N-CDs' potential future as primary research and how that may be used. We hope that this study will contribute to a better understanding of the principles of N-CDs and the sensory applications that they can serve.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Fluorescent properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Scientists have been intrigued by the discovery of carbon dots (CDs) due to their unusual qualities, such as outstanding photoluminescence ( Liu et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2021), minimal or low toxicity (Anwar et al., 2019; Chung et al., 2020), numerous precursor sources (Kurian and Paul, 2021; Perumal et al., 2021), facile surface functionalization (Chen et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2021), and strong biocompatibility (Ji et al., 2020). Zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, such as CDs, are a new member of the family, and they have a diameter under 10 nm. Xu et al. found CDs serendipitously while purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) using electrophoresis. CDs have advanced fast since then, becoming one of the most active study areas in applied materials science, such as optoelectronic devices, biomedicine, biosensors, and catalysis (Fernando et al., 2015; Harris et al., 2016; Hoang et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). There are three types of CDs based on the nature of the carbon source: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Aside from size and photochemical qualities, they vary in internal structure and surface chemical functional groups (Wang et al., 2019).

Photoluminescence (PL), which encompasses fluorescence and phosphorescence, is the most intriguing property of CDs. CDs' structures have yet to be thoroughly studied, but this has not stopped researchers from working hard to develop new methods of synthesizing and using them, notably in biological and pollution inspection applications. When it comes to nano theranostics, CDs are a relatively recent phenomenon. With minimal cytotoxicity and high stability, CDs may be used in live-cell bioimaging to monitor endogenous cellular processes. Recently there has been an emerging trend to achieve near-infrared (NIR) light-emitting CDs for in-vivo imaging applications since NIR light is harmless to live cells.

In recent years, researchers have used a variety of heteroatoms (nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, boron, fluorine, etc.) and metals (Zn, Mg, Ag, Au, Cu, Ga, etc.) as doping agents to improve the physicochemical characteristics of CDs. Doping may modify the intrinsic property of CDs by altering the electrical structure, which can change their fluorescent performance. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap determines the CDs' photophysical characteristics, and this gap may be influenced by varying the kinds and amounts of dopants inserted. Doping atomic impurities may considerably improve these materials' quantum yield (QY), which is why several researchers have recently focused on new dopants and techniques. As a result of these efforts, a wide variety of single- and co-doped CDs have been developed with applications in biosensing, bioimaging, and biomedicine.

Nitrogen is an efficient dopant due to its sizeable atomic size, strong electronegative character, five valence electrons for chemical bonding, and lone pair of electrons that can be readily transported to the orbitals of sp2 carbon structures. As a result, several studies have focused on the effects of nitrogen doping and other modifications on the electrical structure of CDs. Photophysical characteristics of CDs, including their PL, PL shift, and light absorption, are considerably altered by N-doping. Since N-CDs may have varied N-based chemical structures (e.g., pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N), N-doping can result in wide architectures and sometimes unanticipated features.

The synthesis (Bartolomei et al., 2021; Kurian and Paul, 2021; Perumal et al., 2021), functionalization (Park et al., 2016), luminescence processes (Ai et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2015), characteristics, and numerous applications of fluorescent CDs (Gao et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019) have recently been fully described and addressed in many outstanding reviews. Our goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of fluorescent N-CDs and their sensing applications, given their significance in recent times. We will accomplish this by examining the research over the last five years. Firstly, a general introduction to N-CDs (including their production and emission origin) is presented. Following that, the sensor design and the fluorescent sensor applications of N-CDs are provided. There are various sensing targets for N-CDs that make up the essence of this study. The review's focus is on the sensor's performance and its ability to detect a variety of targets. A few exemplary studies are offered here rather than an effort to cover all published in this area. At the conclusion of this review, the merits and disadvantages of N-CDs and their future directions and prospects are analyzed. The review presented here is believed to help design and develop new types of sensors based on fluorescent N-CDs, hence fostering the expansion of their sensing applications.

CQD precursors are generally synthetic or environmentally friendly raw materials, whereas GQDs are often made from graphene (Ai et al., 2021; Bartolomei et al., 2021; Park et al., 2016). GQDs are amorphous quasi-spherical nanodots without quantum confinement, contrasted to crystalline CQDs (Wang et al., 2017). The structure of CQDs may be divided into two groups depending on their core, structures, and morphologies. The core structure can be crystalline (like sp2 carbon) or amorphous, whereas the surface layer can contain varied polar or non-polar groups, from tiny functional groups to lengthy atomic chains. The graphitic form most frequently encountered in the literature is a sphere (He et al., 2022). In recent decades, researchers have made remarkable progress in producing CQDs that are functionally designed to fit the needs of detecting specific analytes (Hoang et al., 2019). Modifying CQDs makes them suitable as platforms for customized sensing by allowing for detecting and attaching specific analytes via electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonds (Hoang et al., 2019).
N-CDs' synthetic routes are categorized into top-down and bottom-up approaches.

In the top-down approach, the desired N-CDs are achieved either by disintegrating the bulk carbon molecules under nitrogen-rich reaction conditions or by disintegrating the bulk nitrogen-doped carbon molecules. The top-down approaches include pulsed laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical, microwave plasma, and hydrothermal cutting routes (shown in Fig. 1 ).

In the pulsed laser ablation approach, the graphitic carbon and nitrogen source are irradiated with an intense laser beam to sublimate the N-CDs. This eco-friendly, rapid, single-step synthesis can be performed at room temperature and pressure and does not require post-purification measures (Novoa-De León et al., 2019; Santiago et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2021). The arc discharge is an affordable method in which the graphite electrode is arced in the presence of hydrogen, helium, and nitrogen gas at 100–150 A discharge current and open circuit potential 60 V (Dey et al., 2014). The electrochemical method comprises an electrochemical cell in which graphene film, Ag/AgCl, and Pt wire act as working electrodes, reference electrodes, and counter electrodes (Li et al., 2012). The electrodes are dipped in tetrabutylammonium perchlorate electrolyte. The N-doped GQDs are obtained by applying potential ±3.0 V at the scan rate of 0.5 V/s. This electrochemical oxidation is also known as the electrochemical cutting technique. Although this is a low-cost method, it produces dissimilar-sized N-CDs and requires further filtration. Microwave plasma is another robust, one-pot top-down synthetic way in which the C60 powder is sublimed in Nitrogen career gas to obtain the N-doped GQDs on Nickel substrate (Zheng et al., 2018). Being rapid, it is a green synthetic route for N-CD preparation. In the hydrothermal cutting route, the scission of peroxidized N-doped graphene into N-doped graphene carbon is achieved by hydrothermal treatment (Ming et al., 2012). This method offers convenient operation for advanced materials synthesis. N-CDs are also prepared by unfolding fullerene by chemical oxidation in the presence of H2O2 and NH4OH at 120 °C in a round bottom flask (Limosani et al., 2021) which is an inexpensive method. In the recent decade, a wide range of bottom-up approaches have been developed to synthesize N-CDs as an alternative approach to conventional top-down routes because most top-down methods are time-consuming, involve complicated operations, costly materials, extremely harsh conditions, and limit the use of diverse precursors.

Achieving the N-CDs with a homogeneous size distribution, less agglomeration with good surface characteristic properties, and scalability is challenging. The bottom-up approach has gained enormous attention in developing CDs in recent years due to its simplicity, ease of operation, low cost, and efficiency in large-scale synthesis. Along with these, it can also adopt diverse precursors in designing the monodisperse N-CDs.

Bottom-up approaches are the most common routes followed for the synthesis of N-CDs. This works on the strategy of blending nitrogen and carbon precursors. The precursors may be either organic chemicals or biomass-derived wastes. The fusion of precursors incorporates dehydration, carbonization, and polymerization. So far, the reported bottom-up methods for preparing N-CDs include pyrolysis, hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, ultrasonic, microwave-assisted hydrothermal, ultrasonic-assisted hydrothermal, and solution plasma, and microwave plasma synthetic methods. Pyrolysis is the traditional way the crucible holding the precursors is pyrolyzed in the furnace at the required temperature to produce N-CDs (Liu et al., 2021; Thongsai et al., 2017). This involves high temperature and acid/alkali conditions for the pyrolysis of precursors. Hydrothermal is one of the most used bottom-up approaches for synthesizing N-CDs. In this method, the non-graphitic carbon precursors and water are subjected to high temperature and pressure in a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber enclosed in a steel jacket. This is a simple, one-pot, facile, versatile, affordable, and eco-friendly approach that furnishes uniform particle size distribution and offers to choose various precursors, including organic chemicals and low-cost bio-wastes for the synthesis of N-CDs (Shen et al., 2021). The major limitation of this method is no control over the size of N-CDs. The solvothermal method is similar to hydrothermal, except non-aqueous solvents are used instead of water (Omer et al., 2018). In microwave-assisted synthesis, the solid organic molecules irradiated with microwave radiation undergo carbonization to yield small N-CDs. This inexpensive, rapid, solvent-free, environmentally safe, one-pot green synthetic method renders good yield (Cao et al., 2019; Ghanem et al., 2020). However, the size of the N-CDs cannot be controlled. The ultrasonic-assisted synthesis is another important low-temperature route for N-CD preparation, which employs the irradiation of highly intense ultrasonic radiation on carbon molecules at room temperature (Xu et al., 2022). Several researchers have recently modified the conventional hydrothermal method by hybridizing microwave with hydrothermal and ultrasonic with the hydrothermal method. In microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis, the hydrothermal autoclave containing precursors is placed in the microwave reaction system at known temperatures (Edison et al., 2016a). In contrast, the autoclave was connected to the transducer of the ultrasonic setup in the ultrasound-assisted method (Qi et al., 2021). These hybrid synthetic routes consume less power. Solution or liquid plasma has recently been developed as an alternative to the high-temperature pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods. The non-equilibrium plasma produced between the electrodes dipped in the precursor solution by applying bipolar-pulsed power induces N-CD formation (Kim et al., 2020). The microwave plasma is yet another rapid, robust, and green way of preparing N-CDs in which the C60 powder is sublimated in a quartz tube using 2.45 GHz microwave radiation and power 1.5 kW. The sublimed C60 in the plasma zone reacts with nitrogen plasma, yielding N-CDs on the Ni collector.

Doping is amongst the most fundamental methods for enhancing PL characteristics in CDs. In particular, new surface defects are created during the doping process for CDs, which results in a rise in energy traps and an increase in overall defects. Consequently, non-radiative electron transfer is less likely to occur, resulting in an increase in the QY of CDs due to this effect. The most common dopant, N atoms, could generally generate a red shift in CD emission wavelengths and an enhancement in fluorescence QY. This is a consequence of the surface energy states induced by the doping of the N atom, which promotes more radiative recombination of excited electrons and less non-radiative recombination. Regarding excited states, amino groups, and other electron-donating N-related functional groups may make excited states more stable by increasing the conjugation degrees in CDs.

Several heteroatom co-doping synergistic impacts improve CD performance significantly over single-heteroatom doping. Recent reports suggest co-doping S and N elements with CDs might improve their fluorescent properties. According to Dong et al., co-doping N and S in CDs resulted in a 73% increase in QY compared to N-CDs and CDs, respectively (Dong et al., 2013). Furthermore, the lifetime of S, N-CDs (12.11 ns) was longer than that of CDs (7.45 ns). They corroborated that the N doping procedure created a new surface state in CDs, and the co-doping of S atoms substantially increased this state's density (Fig. 2 ).

There is a plethora of techniques that might be used to generate PL in CDs nowadays. In the past, it was considered that each form of CDs had its unique mechanism. CDs emit various colors, from deep ultraviolet to near-infrared, although blue is the most frequent emission color. CDs' wide size distribution is the primary reason for the symmetrical and broad spectrum of most PL spectra. In addition, substantial Stokes shifts are observed due to various electronic transition paths. PL spectra often show excitation-dependent behavior, where the emission peak changes with the excited wavelength. Because of these characteristics, CDs seem to be a potential medium for multicolor imaging (Kim et al., 2021; Mintz et al., 2021; Nandi et al., 2021; Paul et al., 2021; Urushihara et al., 2021). Moreover, CDs have a bandgap that is not zero and fluoresces when excited by an external light source. CDs' size and surface chemistry may be modified to alter the band gap (Zhu et al., 2012). A Stokes shift in CDs occurs when surface functional groups oxidize, trapping excitons and red-shifting the emission. Considerable control over multicolor emission may be accomplished by adjusting the chemical process, changing the surface states, and doping (Hu et al., 2015). Despite several in-depth investigations on the mechanics of PL, most findings are inconsistent due to the high number of distinct CDs. However, a number of phenomenological PL processes have been postulated, supported by simplified theoretical models and based on substantial experimental data: (i) PL mostly pertaining to the CD core's -conjugated domains' size; (ii) the doping effects and surface functional groups are responsible for determining the surface state PL; (iii) the molecular PL, which is imprinted solely by fluorescent molecules attached to the surface and/or interior of the CDs. In addition, because of the short singlet-triplet energy splitting (ES-T) in CDs, the participation of triplet states can lead to long-time PL phenomena such as thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and phosphorescence. Since all of these processes may, in theory, occur in separate CD subclasses and work together, it is exceedingly difficult to make sense of PL properties like QYs, (multi-)exponential PL decay, susceptibility to photo-bleaching, and environmental influences. There have been a number of reviews published on the subject of PL of carbon nanostructures (Ai et al., 2021; Langer et al., 2021; Li and Gong, 2022; Qi et al., 2021; Wang and Lu, 2022). The precise mechanism of the fluorescence processes is still up for discussion. Even CDs made by different labs using the same methodology have variable characteristics. Problems arise from the fact that preparation methods differ and well-defined organic CDs are lacking.

There are six different techniques for sensing employing CD-based fluorescent probes. Those are photo-induced electron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE), aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), and aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Several research groups have thoroughly examined and reviewed the sensing mechanisms of CDs (Hu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021b; Sun and Lei, 2017).

Fluorescent CDs-based sensors may be designed using three different approaches. One is where, analytes directly interact with CDs, causing changes in fluorescence signals from these dots. For example, CDs created in the early stages were used as metal ion sensors where the CDs surface may firmly and precisely interact with the target metal ion (Chen et al., 2016; Edison et al., 2016b; Zhou et al., 2015). Fluorescence quenching is a common phenomenon in CD-based metal ion detection. In fluorescence quenching, the analyte-induced non-radiative e−/h+ annihilation of charge carriers in photo-excited CDs is the basis for metal ions detection. Effective quenching can be induced by binding these ions to the carboxyl-rich surfaces of CQDs. IFE is related to CQD fluorescence quenching in a general sense. CD optical probes can be attributed to several processes and factors, like primary and secondary inner filter effects (IFEs), analyte ion-activated non-radiative recombination, and analyte-ion chelating interactions (Wang et al., 2010). This method is commonly employed in the design of CD-based metal ion sensing platforms for Cu(II) and Fe(III) ions. These ions can be bound to carboxyl surface-rich CDs to induce effective quenching. In a broad sense, the fluorescence quenching of CDs is allied with IFE. This phenomenon frequently occurs when the detecting system uses absorbers and fluorophores whose excitation and emission spectra overlap. As a result, it is crucial to select an appropriate absorber and fluorophore coupling for the IFE-based fluorescent sensory systems.

The second most important technique is the post-functionalization of CDs. To build fluorescent nanosensors with great sensitivity and specificity, it is well-regarded that particular receptors must be conjugated with CDs. A variety of sensors were developed by incorporating functional groups, such as thiols (Chen et al., 2021), boronic acid (Ngo et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2017), peptides (Bhunia et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2018), and amino acids (Pandit et al., 2019) on the surface of CDs. In particular, CDs were functionalized with boronic acid groups on the surface to generate a range of glucose sensors since the boronic acid motif has demonstrated a great affinity and remarkable selectivity towards carbohydrates such as glucose.

The third one is where CDs are integrated with other materials (such as quenchers, fluorophores, substrates, etc.). For example, a complex of quenched CDs' fluorescence is restored when the quenchers and analytes are broken apart and recombined. The fluorescence of CDs decreases following the addition of various metal ions. This quenched complex might detect anions and other analytes (Liang et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2021; Zhang and Fan, 2020; Zhang et al., 2021).

The CDs developed under the second and third categories work under the PET and FRET principles. Essentially, these techniques for sensing applications entail intermolecular interactions between CDs and target molecules.

Notably, CDs with ratiometric fluorescence sensing are the most useful. Since ratiometric sensing relies on the fluorescence intensity ratio at two or more wavelengths, it is more reliable and less prone to interference (Huang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Liang et al. (2022); Qin et al. (2021); Shi et al. (2021); Wang et al., 2022; Wang et al. (2016); Zhai et al. (2018); Zhang et al. (2021); Zhao et al. (2022); Zhu et al. (2021).

Recently, many papers have been reported on CD-based nanoprobes for fluorescence turn-on and turn-off assays for cations. Iron ions have recently been the most extensively reported metal ion target using fluorescent CDs. Iron insufficiency is one of the most common micronutrient deficits globally, accounting for one-third of all micronutrient deficiencies. In 2022, Krishnaiah et al. reported blue emissive N-CDs for Fe3+ detection (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). Fe3+ ions quench the as-prepared N-CDs emission, and the quenching rate was linear with Fe3+ ion concentration. Fe3+ ions have a limit of detection (LOD) value of 1.4 μM, with a detection range of 5.0–25 μM, according to their study (Fig. 3 a). Natural bauhinia flower carbon was used by Huang et al. in a simple and environmentally friendly microwave technique to produce N-CDs (Huang et al., 2018). The N-CDs were shown to be an effective “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for detecting Fe3+, showing good sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.01 μM (Fig. 3b). In 2017, Krishnaiah et al. developed an ultra-bright N/P co-doped carbon nanoprobe for detecting Fe3+ in biological samples (Shangguan et al., 2017). The N/P co-doped CDs could be easily synthesized via hydrothermal treatment using adenosine five ′ triphosphates (ATP) as an N/P source. As the quantity of Fe3+ was increased, a fluorescence quenching response was seen in the as-fabricated CDs (Fig. 3c). With a detection limit of 0.33 μM, the calibration curve showed a wide linear area across 1–150 μM.

Various research groups have used this sensing process to build Fe3+ sensors on fluorescent CDs manufactured using various precursors or synthetic approaches. Due to the varied precursors employed in synthesis, the CDs have diverse surface chemical groups, binding characteristics, and sensitivity and selectivity. Hydrothermal, microwave, solid-phase, pyrolysis, acid vapor cutting, and electrochemical procedures were used to make these Fe3+ sensors. Table 1 summarizes the reports N-CDs production, fluorescent characteristics, and sensor performance on Fe3+.

Copper ion (Cu2+) is an essential mineral in all sorts of water. When consumed in large doses, it is toxic and may cause damage to the liver and kidneys. Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease has been related to Cu2+. We may learn more about how Cu2+ ions affect health and disease if we keep track of them. With o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as a precursor and Al(NO3)3–9H2O as an assistant, Yang's group reported red dual-emissive CDs, which can efficiently detect Cu2+ via emission quenching. (Fig. 4 a) (Yang et al., 2021). Ge et al. synthesized strong green-emitting N-CDs with a 51.2% quantum yield via the hydrothermal treatment of hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors. Their report indicates that Cu2+ can quench N-CDs' fluorescence by a factor of six-fold, which can be utilized for Cu2+ assays ranging from 0.05 to 5 μM with a LOD of 25 nM (Fig. 4b). Zhang et al. reported a simple ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal Cu2+ detection system based on N-CDs produced hydrothermally in one step (Fig. 4c). The approach involves electrostatically adsorbing 2,3-diamino phenazine (oxOPD), the oxidation product of OPD, on the surface of N-CDs, thereby quenching their fluorescence while providing a new emission peak at 553 nm (Zhang et al., 2019).

Mercury (II) ion (Hg2+) is one of the most harmful and pervasive contaminants. Hg2+ is known to readily travel through epidermal, respiratory, and gastrointestinal tissues, causing DNA damage, mitotic impairment, and lasting DNA damage. The maximum Hg2+ in drinking water is 10 nM, according to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Using a one-step hydrothermal technique, Liao's team developed inexpensive and simple fluorescent N-CDs from citric acid and ethanolamine (Fig. 5 a) (Liao et al., 2019). Hg2+ concentrations can be quickly and sensitively determined using this N-CDs fluorescent probe with a linear range of 0.1–5.0 μM and a detection limit of 0.019 μM. Recently, Muthurasu et al. has reported red emissive N-CDs by altering the surface of the N-CDs by incorporating Rhodamine B. This fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based N-CDS showed ratiometric sensing behavior in the presence of Hg2+ in an aqueous medium (Fig. 5b) (Muthurasu and Ganesh, 2021). Given the large number of similar studies conducted, we have compiled a list of some of the more notable ones in Table 2 .

Other metal ions, such as Cr6+, Pb2+, Co2+, In3+, Ca2+, and so on, have also been identified using fluorescent CDs. Diverse metal ions were detected using various techniques and sensory phenomena or sensitivities. N-CDs derived from the biomass of Poa Pratensis have been used to detect Mn2+ via the quenching method (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). Chromic exposure to Cr6+ has a corrosive impact on human health; hence new detection techniques have been developed. Fluorescent N-CDs to detect Cr6+ are growing in popularity (Das and Dutta, 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Ming et al., 2019). For example, Kaur et al. have reported N-CDs using L-ascorbic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors (Kaur et al., 2020). The blue emitting as prepared N-CDs tend to quench in the presence of Cr6+ in aqueous conditions. The fluorescence calibration curve was linear in the range of 0–4 μM, and the LOD of 2.59 nM was determined from the slope of the linear curve. Zhang et al. realized ratiometric detection of Cr6+ using N-CDs (Zhang et al., 2021). A similar pernicious element Pb2+ has also been detected by N-CDs (Bandi et al., 2018). The N-CDs developed from Lantana camara berries have detected Pb2+ with very low LOD (9.64 nM). N-CDs derived from freeze-dried tofu could detect Co2+, and their effective bioimaging was also examined (Wen et al., 2021). Pawar et al. have developed red-emitting CDs for dual sensing of indium (In3+) and palladium Pb2+ in water (Pawar et al., 2020). In3+ detection was related to a “turn-on” fluorescence response with a redshift, while Pd2+ detection was associated with a “turn-off” fluorescence response. For the detection of calcium ions (Ca2+), Yue and his groups produced surface-modified N-CDs containing ethylenebis (oxyethylenenitrilo)tetraacetic acid (EGTA) (Yue et al., 2019). In the presence of Ca2+, the bright blue fluorescence of EGTA-modified N-CDs subsides.

The use of CDs for anion detection was much less common than the detection of metal ions. Metal ions (e.g., Fe3+, Cu2+) have been shown to turn-off the fluorescence of CDs. Adding anions can revive the fluorescence that has been quenched since some of them can form a stronger complex with these cations. Fluorescence-on phenomena were used to detect anion concentrations such as PO4 3−, S2−, and NO2 −, among others.

Ge et al. have developed N-CDs from hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors whose quenched strong green emission could be recovered by S2− (Ge et al., 2021). Xu et al. reported polyethylene glycol (PEG) for detecting S2−. Using a double ratiometric fluorescence approach, this is one of the rare instances in which an anion is recognized directly by CDs without the need for cations (Y. Xu et al., 2021). To detect PO4 3−, Gao et al. developed yellow fluorescent N-CDs by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and 4-aminobutyric acid. Combining N-CDs with Cu2+ in a coordination complex was the basis of this approach. Using competitive complexation, PO4 3− can trap Cu2+ on N-CDs' surface, resulting in fluorescence and UV–vis absorption (Gao et al., 2021). Several N-CDs capable of detecting nitrite anion (NO2−) have recently been identified. For example, Jana et al. synthesized fluorescent N-CDs using 3-aminophenyboronic acid and aqueous citric acid, and the as-synthesized CDs showed selective and sensitive NO2 − detection. Wang et al. synthesized N, and B-doped CDs using a facile hydrothermal process. Because of the diazotization interaction between NO2 − and their N-CDs' surface amino group, its innate fluorescence was quenched (Wang et al., 2022). Zhang et al. have constructed a novel hybrid nanosystem consisting of CDs and curcumin for sensitive and selective sensing of F−. The curcumin-F– combination is generated when F− is introduced, causing an absorption band hypochromic shift from 532 to 430 nm (Zhang et al., 2021).

Using p-phenylenediamine as a starting material, Yue et al. produced an off-on fluorescence probe based on ferric functionalized CDs to detect superoxide anion (O2 •−) (Yue et al., 2021). Şenol and Onganer successfully synthesized N-CDs using a hydrothermal treatment of cranberry as a novel natural resource (Şenol and Onganer, 2022). ClO− oxidizes the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs− ions, which quench N-CDs' green fluorescence, and a LOD for ClO− was computed as 1.60 nM. Wang et al. functionalized N-CDs with aminophenylboronic acid to generate ClO− detecting N-CDs (Wang et al., 2021). ClO−, unlike other interferents, has the potential to disrupt CD surface passivation when used as an oxidant. In their hypothesis, the oxidation of the surface hydroxyl groups on CDs causes their fluorescence response to ClO−. This hypothesis was later confirmed experimentally. Li et al. reported N-CDs-based fluorescent and colorimetric probes for visually, sensitively, and selectively sensing benzoyl peroxide (Li et al., 2021a). Their N-CDs detect benzoyl peroxide via a “turn-off” mechanism.

N-CDs have lately been used to detect a slew of possible biomarkers and food additives in addition to cations and anions. Small molecules like ascorbic acid, glutathione (GSH), and cysteine (Cys) may form strong complexes with metals and reverse the fluorescence of N-CDs quenched by cations, much as anions do.

AA is a powerful antioxidant and free radical scavenger in food, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. Scurvy, iron deficiency anemia, and atherosclerosis are all caused by low AA levels in the body. Thus, a simple and efficient AA detection analytical approach is critical for early and quick illness identification (Ji et al., 2021; Li et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2018). Zhang et al. synthesized N-CDs by comprising pyrolysis of citric acid with glutamic acid (Zhang et al., 2018) (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence of their N-CDs quenches in the presence of Cr6+. Due to AA's ability to convert Cr6+ into trivalent chromium (Cr(III)), the fluorescence intensity in the N-CDs/Cr6+ system could quickly recover in the presence of AA. In addition, a linear range for AA detection was found from 1 to 750 μM with a LOD of 0.3 μM. Tammina and Yang utilized a similar detection method to detect AA by the N-CDs developed from glucosamine (Tammina and Yang, 2020) (Fig. 6b). The N-CDs developed by Xu et al. detect AA by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ and efficaciously recover the quenched fluorescence of N-CDs/Fe3+ (Xu et al., 2021) (Fig. 6c).

GSH is essential in supporting immune system activity and creating a reduced environment in cells. It minimizes oxidative damage caused by free radicals and reactive oxygen species and regulates intracellular redox homeostasis, making it an essential human biomarker. Thus, GSH detection is vital and extensively studied (Li et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019). The oxidative ability of Ag+ towards N-CDs generated by solvothermal means was used by Chen et al. to create a new, simple nanosensor for determining Ag+ and GSH (Fig. 7 a) (Chen et al., 2021). The redox interactions between Ag+ and N-CDs could be suppressed when GSH was injected into the solution, preventing N-CDs' fluorescence from being enhanced since thiols could form Ag–S bonds with Ag+. Similarly, Jiao et al. created N-CDs capable of detecting GSH when the N-CDs interacted with Ag+ (Fig. 7b) (Jiao et al., 2019). Jia et al. used phenylenediamines as carbon sources to make three N-CDs in a one-step solvothermal technique. Colorimetric and fluorescence dual modes are used to detect GSH over Cys, homocysteine (Hcy), amino acids, and ions in the red-emitting CDs (p-CDs) (Fig. 7c) (Jia et al., 2020).

A similar function to GSH is played by Cys, which is an essential component of the body's antioxidant defenses. To detect Cys, Liang et al. devised a quenched CDs Rhodamine B system, in which Hg2+-quenched CDs serve as the reporting unit, and Rhodamine B serves as the reference unit (Liang et al., 2019). Their study found a Cys detection limit of 52.10 nM, much lower than the average concentration of Cys in human urine. Huang et al. reported that the water-soluble N-CDs made from tartaric acid, citric acid, and ethanediamine using a simple one-pot solvothermal process to detect Hg2+ and Cys in an “off-on” manner (Huang et al., 2017). Other researchers have utilized a similar fluorescence reversal approach to identify Cys ( Liu et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021).

Glucose transport in the human body has been linked to diabetes and various malignancies, making glucose monitoring vital for illness prevention, diagnosis, and therapy. The use of boronic acid in identifying and detecting glucose has grown since boronic acid-containing molecules have a significant affinity and specificity for sugars like glucose. Boronic acid may create cyclic boronate ester with 1,2- and 1,3-diols. A series of N-CDs containing boronic acid functional groups were created using this technique. 3-Aminophenyl boronic acid and sodium citrate were used as precursors by Zou et al. to synthesize N-CDs utilizing a one-step “synthesis-modification integration” technique (Fig. 8 a) (Zou et al., 2018). The boronic acid groups on the N-CDs surface may combine with the cis-diols group of glucose to produce a coordination complex, resulting in CD fluorescence quenching due to aggregation states. Besides boronic acid-based glucose detection, other principles have also been exploited. For example, Liu et al. developed N-CDs whose fluorescence emission was quenched in Ag+ ions (Fig. 8b) (Liu et al., 2020). Fluorescence that has been quenched may be quantitatively restored by adding glucose, which has a low LOD (0.375 μM) and a wide linear range (0.1–1000 μM).

An important monoamine neurotransmitter, DA, is produced by the pars compacta of the subsynaptic nucleus and plays a vital role in many physiological activities of both human beings and animals. Lan et al. fabricated the N-CDs + Fe3+ system for fluorescence turn-on detection of DA (Lan et al., 2019). According to Fig. 9 a, the CDs contain an abundance of phenol (Ar-OH), which may combine with Fe3+ to produce the Fe3+-OAr complex. However, the dihydroxy group in DA has a greater affinity for Fe3+ than the N-CDs do. Fe3+ was released off the N-CDs' surface once DA was applied, allowing the fluorescence to be recovered. There is high selectivity for DA in the N-CDs + Fe3+ system, and the LOD is 0.18 μM. Zhang et al. prepared N-CDs which can easily be quenched by DA oxidation product dopaquinone with the addition of tyrosinase (Tyr) (Fig. 9b). Given the above mechanism, the proposed biosensor was utilized to detect DA with LOD as low as 0.035 μM(Zhang and Fan, 2020). Naik et al. demonstrated N-CDs' preparation via hydrothermal synthesis as a naked-eye fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of DA (Naik et al., 2019) (Fig. 9c). Loul et al. developed a fluorometric method for determining DA in human fluids at nanomolar concentrations (Fig. 9d) (Louleb et al., 2020).

As a crucial component of cell function, ATP serves as a major energy source, a signaling molecule inside the cell, a catalyst for DNA/RNA synthesis, and a trigger for the synthesis of amino acids, among other functions. According to Huang and his group (Huang et al., 2018), the microwave-treated bloom of the Bauhinia plant produced blue luminous N-CDs. Because of the high affinity between Fe3+ and oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the CDs, the fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively quenched by Fe3+ through an electron transfer mechanism. The N-CDs' fluorescence was restored with the addition of ATP, which has a greater affinity for Fe3+. The N-CDs + Fe3+ system has a LOD of 5 nM to ATP.

Antibiotics are a class of chemical substances commonly employed in agriculture, animal husbandry, aquaculture, and medicine to treat bacterial infections. Allergy responses, resistance to germs, and an increased risk of cancer have been linked to leftover antibiotics and their metabolites. Increasing emphasis has been paid to the threat posed by residual drugs and bacterial antibiotic resistance. As a result, monitoring antibiotics is essential for reducing the environmental dangers associated with antibiotic pollution.

Gunjal et al. used waste tea residue to synthesize fluorescent N-CDs using a simple, low-cost, and one-step chemical oxidation method (Gunjal et al., 2019). Fluorescent characteristics, surface functionality, and particle size of N-CDs were measured. The CDs were highly water-soluble and bioimaging friendly. Due to the inner filter effect (IFE), the CDs' fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing tetracycline (TC) concentration (Fig. 10 a). Xing and colleagues used thiourea and citric acid as precursors in a hydrothermal technique to efficiently synthesize N-CDs (Xing et al., 2020). The fluorometric detection of TCs was performed using N-CDs synthesized in an aqueous solution. The method was based on N-CDs' fluorescence at 440 nm being selectively quenched by TCs through the IFE process (Fig. 10b). It is also possible to employ TCs' fluorescence as an internal standard signal with a maximum of 600 nm. For TC, doxycycline (DC), and oxytetracycline (OTC), the LOD was 0.25, 0.39, and 0.26 nM, respectively. Using p-dihydroxybenzene and hydrazine hydrate, Fu and associates created N-CDs exhibiting blue fluorescence (Fig. 10c) (Fu et al., 2020). The strong metal-ligand coordination and electrostatic interactions between the significant quantity of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the N-CDs surface and Fe3+ allowed for selective quenching of CD fluorescence. With a LOD of 0.70 μM, this non-fluorescent N-CDs + Fe3+ compound turned on fluorescence when exposed to ampicillin. An N-CD nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions and ciprofloxacin in the presence of bipyridine was developed by Dang et al. (2020). As shown in Fig. 10d, N-CDs are made by hydrothermally treating citric acid and ethylenediamine. The substantial charge transfer between Cu2+ ions and N-CDs quenches N-CDs' fluorescence in the presence of Cu2+ ions. Bipyridine-Cu@N-CDs have ultrahigh sensitivity for ciprofloxacin detection in the concentration range of 0.05–50 μM. Cu2+ ions operate as an analyte and a fluorescence quencher, with bipyridine as a linker between Cu2+ and ciprofloxacin.
Given the large number of similar studies conducted, we have compiled a list of some of the more notable ones in Table 3 .

As a typical hazardous substance, nitroaromatic explosives pose environmental, health, and homeland security risks. Thus, developing quick and sensitive detection methods for nitroaromatic explosives is critical. The detection of nitro explosives has previously been the subject of several high-quality experiments. Fan et al. created new water-soluble and blue-fluorescent N-CDs using malonic acid and urea in a one-pot hydrothermal technique, which was subsequently employed as a label-free fluorescent probe for picric acid (PA) detection (Fan et al., 2017). PA was able to selectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs within 10 s. The fluorescence intensity of the probe had a linear response to PA concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 26.5 μM, with a low detection limit of 51 nM (Fig. 11 a).

Li et al. developed N and P-codoped N-CDs by separately employing adenosine, adenosine monophosphate, adenosine diphosphate, and adenosine-5′-triphosphate as precursors (Li et al., 2018). N-CDs fluorescence can be quenched in seconds by PA, allowing for a PA concentration measurement down to 30 nM. Inner filter effect and static quenching were explored as potential mechanisms for fluorescence quenching (Fig. 11b). In a different work, grape skin was used as a raw material by a one-step hydrothermal method to produce fluorescence N-CDs (Li et al., 2018). PA quenched the fluorescence of the carbon nanodots in the phosphate buffer solution at pH = 7.4, and a technique for detecting PA with excellent sensitivity and selectivity was developed (Fig. 11c). Zhang and co-workers developed yellow-emitting N-CDs for the selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) (Zhang et al., 2020). N-CDs' fluorescence was linearly related to the TNP concentration (0.2–130 μM), with a detection limit of 56 nM (Fig. 11d).

An essential component of the contemporary food business is pesticides. After fertilizers, these compounds are the second most important class of artificial chemicals to have changed agriculture by increasing yields and revenues. Pesticides and veterinary medications are often used to manage weeds and pests, as well as to regulate plant development and to prevent and cure illness in livestock. Food and environmental contamination from excessive use or misuse of pesticides or veterinary drugs pose a risk to human health. As a result, pesticide and veterinary medicine residue detection methods are essential.

For the first time, Liu et al. developed a highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for methyl parathion (MP) by employing alkaline-induced hydrolysis (Liu et al., 2022). Hydrothermal heating was used to synthesize the N-CDs from o-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine as precursors. As MP hydrolyzes in an alkaline environment, the p-NP formed is able to block the N-fluorescence CD's by combining IFE and dynamic interaction. MP sensing was achieved for the N-CDs with a linear range of 0.01–15.0 μg/mL and a sensitivity of 1.56 ng/mL (Fig. 12 a).

Li et al. created label-free N-CDs based on acetylcholinesterase (AChE)-controlled quenching of fluorescent CDs for sensitive detection of organophosphate pesticides (OPs) using dual-mode (fluorometric and colorimetric) channels (Li et al., 2018). When AChE and acetylthiocholine react, they generate thiocholine, which mainly triggers the breakdown of 5,5-dithiobis (a yellow-colored 2-nitrobenzoic acid) (TNBA). The fluorescence of CDs could be quenched dynamically by using TNBA, which has a positive charge as a potent absorber. The presence of OPs inhibited the AChE enzyme activity, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence signal and a reduction in absorbance intensity with color variation (Fig. 12b).

Li et al. developed a novel technique for achieving effective red emissive N-CDs emitting at 610 nm (fluorescence quantum yield of around 24.0%) utilizing solvothermal treatment of citric acid and thiourea in dimethylformamide as the solvent (Li et al., 2020). N-CDs were considered a simple ratiometric fluorescent substrate for assessing trace amounts of OPs due to their fluorescent characteristics and abundance of surface functional groups. They combined the polymerization of dopamine by acetylcholinesterase with the inhibition of the enzyme by pesticides. The degree of polymerization of dopamine is logically dependent on the concentration of OPs. By analyzing the fluorescence intensity ratio, the proposed platform demonstrated extremely selective and robust identification of OPs at the pg L−1 level (Fig. 12c).

TYR, a copper-containing enzyme, is found in animals and plants. As an oxidase, TYR plays a critical role in melanin formation in the organism, insect cuticle sclerotization, and plant browning responses. Zhang et al. have reported amine-rich N-CDs using a nitrogen doping process (Zhang et al., 2019). The impact of nitrogen doping on the responsiveness of various CDs to TYR activity sensing utilizing DA as a substrate was examined. The DA was oxidized to o-dopaquinone via TYR catalysis, and the fluorescence of the N-CDs was quenched by o-dopaquinone. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was directly employed to monitor the TYR activity by employing a predetermined concentration of DA and other optimal reaction conditions.

AChE is a crucial enzyme in the central nervous system of animals, where it catalyzes the hydrolysis process of acetylcholine (ACh) to chorine. To measure AChE and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) activity in human whole blood, Xu et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N-CDs (Xu et al., 2019). Due to the inner filter effect, when Cu2+ oxidized o-phenylenediamine, the product 2,3-diamino phenazine efficiently quenched N-CDs' fluorescence at 460 nm and gave rise to a new emission peak at 570 nm. The hydrolysis process of acetylthiocholine or butyrylthiocholine mediated by AChE or BChE to form thiocholine, whose sulfhydryl group significantly grabbed Cu2+ to block the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine, thus retaining N-CDs fluorescence emission. Cholinesterase (ChEs) activity was detected using the probe's fluorescence intensity ratio as the output signal (Fig. 13 ).

Huang et al. devised a way to make a ratiometric fluorescent probe that was very specific and sensitive for AChE. They used the inner filter effect (IFE) between N-CDs and 2,3-diamino phenazine (DAP) to make the probe (Huang et al., 2019). N-CDs were made by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin and 1,2-ethylenediamine. N-CDs have good fluorescence properties and no cytotoxicity on human cervical cancer HeLa cells and human embryonic kidney 293 T cells, which suggests that they could be used in other ways. Acetylcholine was made into choline when AChE and choline oxidase were added. Then, choline was oxidized to create H2O2. DAP was formed when o-phenylenediamine interacted with H2O2 in the presence of horseradish peroxidase. Since N-CDs have a fluorescence signal at 450 nm and DAP has a 574 nm fluorescence signal, an IFE-based ratiometric fluorescent probing platform exists. The catalytic activity of AChE was irreversibly inhibited by OP, resulting in a drop in DAP and a change in the ratiometric fluorescent signal. AChE may be detected with a sensitivity as low as 0.38 U/L using this fluorescent probe under ideal circumstances (Fig. 14 ).

ALP, found in all human tissues, may catalyze the hydrolysis of nucleic acids, proteins, and certain small compounds. The aberrant expression level of ALP in serum is a key indicator of various illnesses, including breast and prostate cancer, bone disease, lever dysfunction, and diabetes. As a result, the use of fluorescent CDs for sensitive ALP detection is essential. Zhang and coworkers created N-CDs by hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid as a carbon source and glycine as a nitrogen source (Zhang et al., 2019). Then, utilizing N-CDs as a fluorescence probe, they created a straightforward fluorescence approach for the sensitive and selective detection of ALP. As illustrated in Fig. 15 , N-CDs were directly used for signal readout without any modification. The absorption band of PNPP, which differs from that of PNP under alkaline circumstances and coincides well with the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, was selected as an ALP substrate. As a result, PNP through IFE efficiently quenches N-CDs' fluorescence, allowing for real-time monitoring of ALP based on the association between relative fluorescence intensity and ALP activity.

Bacterial infections pose a serious hazard to human safety and health. Studies using nanoparticles as imaging and detection agents have shown enormous promise for treating infectious illnesses. A novel and revolutionary nanoparticle, N-CDs have gained much interest because of their unique fluorescent and physicochemical features and greater biosafety than other nanoparticles. As a result, N-CDs are emerging as the best imaging and detection technology for harmful microorganisms. The fluorescence and surface features of N-CDs are critical for monitoring and battling harmful microorganisms.

The fluorescence features of bacteria have been tagged. After that, the fluorescence intensity is used to calculate the bacteria count to meet the detection objective. Direct labeling with CDs is essential for aspects like fluorescence properties and surface groups, among other things. For example, N-CDs were prepared to label Klebsiella, E. coli, Staphylococcus, and Pseudomonas (Fig. 16 a) (Pathak et al., 2019). Pathak et al. reported a pH-sensitive detection of the pathogenic bacteria using multicolor emissive N-CDs (Fig. 16b) (Pathak et al., 2020). Many harmful bacteria might be detected by their N-CDs, including Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), and Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris). Zeta potential values were used to describe the interaction between N-CDs and bacteria. At ex = 340 nm, researchers examined the PL emission response of bacteria labeled with N-CD. E. coli and S. aureus might be tagged, although E. coli cells had a superior response. As multicolor fluorescent probes, the N-CDs created by Sun et al. might likewise be employed for bacterial imaging. According to their research, N-CDs might be a potential material for biological applications (Fig. 16c) (Sun et al., 2021). N-CDs produced from cysteamine have recently been explored for imaging pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells (Pathak et al., 2021). A pH-sensitive fluorescent N-CDs-based identification technique for E. coli and S. aureus driven by sugar metabolism has been published by Zhao et al. (Fig. 16d) (Zhao et al., 2020).

It has been demonstrated that a wide range of toxic pollutants, including metal cations (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, …), several anions (S2−, NO2 -, …), bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella), and other substances (pesticides, antibiotics, etc.), can be detected in the aquatic environment using N-CDs, with detection limits ranging from ppb to ppm. Notably, there have been reports on applying N-CDs for detecting radioactive heavy metal ions. For instance, Xiaohong's research group developed N-doped CDs that detect uranyl ions (UO2 2+) with a low detection limit of 20.38 nM (Wang et al., 2022). Recently, N-CD-based pollution detection has been applied to new environments, such as the detection of nitrogen dioxide and methane in the air and the detection of alachlor and trifluralin herbicides in the soil. N-CDs have also been a helpful probe for identifying several food-borne contaminants. Hydrothermal treatment of crayfish shells, an otherwise worthless food waste, was used by Chen and coworkers to synthesize N-CDs (Chen et al., 2021) successfully. The N-CDs were then utilized as a toxicology fluorescence probe for thiamphenicol. The detection limits for thiamphenicol in water and actual foods (fish and milk) were determined to be 11.12 and 27.5 μg/L, respectively.

Doping CDs with additional elements (such as nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus) effectively enhanced photoluminescence. This review paper deals with the topic of N-CDs. We discussed the synthesis methods for N-CDs as well as their fluorescent properties. Also discussed are the most current uses of N-CDs in various disciplines, with some practical examples. Our survey of the recently reported N-CDs revealed that N-CDs are the most often used doped-CDs for metal ion detection, particularly Fe3+. Additionally, we observed that hydrothermal synthesis is the most often reported technique for producing N-CDs. It is worth noting that the blue or green emission seen in most N-CDs could very well overlap with organisms' natural background biofluorescence. Furthermore, N-CDs detect analytes in fluorescence “turn-off” mode in the majority of situations.

The advantages of CDs over organic dyes and classical semiconductor QD probes include ease of synthesis/surface functionalization, adjustable composition, acceptable photochemical and physicochemical stability and photostability, solubility in aqueous conditions, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and low environmental impact. Doped CDs have been discovered to be an ideal choice for developing numerous multifunctional nano systems apart from sensory applications. For example, ROS-producing materials, materials that convert light into heat, and nanocarriers for drug delivery, to name a few.

Apart from doping, carbon nanoparticle morphologies, sizes, shapes, and surface modifications must be precisely regulated and optimized to provide superior biocompatibility, long-term stability, and accurate in vivo targeting. In the future, research needs to concentrate on synthesizing CDs that have a broad visible emission range. Several previous papers created CDs with various emission colors; some of these dots exhibited excitation-dependent emission features, but their quantum yield reduced dramatically when the excitation was changed. We expect that a wide variety of CDs could be obtained through modifying molecular precursors and doping agents and would pave the way for a wide variety of uses beyond sensing, such as bioimaging, disease diagnosis, catalysis, energy storage, etc. Improving the quantum yields of CDs in the deep-red or NIR region, where novel synthetic techniques are currently in intense research, is the fundamental hurdle for most of these applications.

Increasing sensitivity while preserving selectivity is a significant task for sensing applications. Precursor selection is one method for accomplishing this objective, as shown in N-CDs-based metal ion detection. Besides, surface modification of CDs with specific ligands effectively produces sensory materials with a range of acceptable targets. For example, CDs with boronic acid groups might be glucose sensors. Most importantly, the primary “on-off” strategy, the CD-based fluorescence detection method, should expand to spectral shift, lifespan variations, ratiometric sensing, and bi-modular detection. Materials modeling can demonstrate the association between material structure, characteristics, and photoluminescence processes; however, it is seldom used in CD design/selection. With increasingly precise models highlighting structure-property-function relationships, fluorescent CDs will play a key role in future CD research and applications. CDs have seldom been created or selected using materials simulation. More detailed models of the structure-property-function relationship are likely needed for fluorescent CDs and their sensory applications in future studies.

CD-based sensing applications are currently being developed and tested in the laboratory. Fluorescent CDs might be used in various commercial and industrial applications in the near future. Due to their high-water solubility, CDs might accumulate in aquatic environments and the human body, despite their purported advantages like minimal toxicity and biocompatibility (organs and tissues). Studies on CD nano-toxicity and green CD synthesis are therefore greatly desired.

Ramyakrishna Pothu: Review and editing. Rajender Boddula: Conceptualization-review & editing and supervision.
